citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee has a bottle of norocillin that is being stored at room temperature. At the time of inspection it was 76 degrees
Farenheit. The directions on the bottle require it to be stored at refrigeration temperatures of 36-46 degrees Fahrenheit.
The licensee has used this bottle on a regulated dog. Not storing medications at the temperature that is required on the
label can result in the medication to become chemically altered and go bad. The medication can become toxic and/or
ineffective. This can result in the dog possibly becoming sick from the medication or the medication not having the desired
effect of treatment for the dog. All medications must be stored in accordance with what is documented on the label.
Correct starting on 5/15/26.

A doggy door was broken/chewed up and half of it was missing. The chewed up edges don't allow for the doggy door to
be properly cleaned and sanitized. In addition, there is a big gap that could make it difficult to keep the cold out of that
enclosure in the winter months. Housing facilities for dogs must be designed and constructed so they are structurally
sound. They must be kept in good repair.
During inspection, a dog was able to leave one enclosure and enter another enclosure. While the the licensee and I didn't
observe it, we believe the dog hopped over the separating wall of the enclosure. I did observe the one enclosure to have
only two dogs one minute and then a minute later the enclosure had three dogs in it. Having enclosures that don't contain
the animals securely or restrict other animals from entering it could result in dogs getting injured when they are moving
(jumping over the wall) from one enclosure to the other. Also, a dog entering an enclosure it wasn't intended to be in
doesn't ensure that dog will be compatible with the dogs that are in that enclosure that it moved (jumped) into. This could
result in a dog fight and potential injuries or even the death of a dog. The licensee must ensure the enclosures contain all
the dogs securely and restrict other animals from entering.
Correct by 5/16/26.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Feces was accumulating throughout most of the enclosures throughout the kennel. When asked, the licensee stated they
were last cleaned two days ago. The accumulation of feces can contribute to the spread of disease. Excreta must be
removed from the primary enclosures daily.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
At least three doggy doors had grime built up on them. At least three enclosures containing nursing dogs and their
puppies had grime and feces material smeared onto the floor within the enclosure. The licensee wasn't sure exactly when
the enclosures were last sanitized but thought it was pushing two weeks. Grime built up on the doggy doors and fecal
material smeared onto the floor of female dogs and their puppies can contribute to the spread of disease. Used primary
enclosures must be sanitized every two weeks or more often as necessary to prevent the accumulation of dirt, debris,
excreta, and other disease hazards. Correct by 5/18/26.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Three empty enclosures had mouse droppings scattered throughout the enclosures. The licensee is not currently using
those enclosures. The licensee stated he had a mouse problem months ago, but has taken care of that and this feces
accumulation is from the mouse problem he had months ago. Not cleaning up old mouse feces can contribute to the
spread of disease within the kennel. All housing facilities must be kept clean to help reduce/eliminate the spread of
disease. Correct by 5/18/26.

None of the adult dogs are vaccinated against distemper. In addition, none of the adult dogs have been sampled for
heartworm or fecal parasites (the program of veterinary care calls for 10% of the dogs to be sampled). Not vaccinating
against distemper and sampling for heartworm or fecal parasites doesn't ensure the population of dogs is adequately
protected against those diseases/parasites. The licensee must vaccinate all dogs against distemper and sample the
population for heartworm and fecal parasites in accordance with a schedule approved by the attending veterinarian.
Correct by 6/15/26.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
End of report.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee has a bottle of norocillin that is being stored at room temperature. At the time of inspection it was 76 degrees 
Farenheit. The directions on the bottle require it to be stored at refrigeration temperatures of 36-46 degrees Fahrenheit. 
The licensee has used this bottle on a regulated dog. Not storing medications at the temperature that is required on the 
label can result in the medication to become chemically altered and go bad. The medication can become toxic and/or 
ineffective. This can result in the dog possibly becoming sick from the medication or the medication not having the desired 
effect of treatment for the dog. All medications must be stored in accordance with what is documented on the label. 
 
Correct starting on 5/15/26. 
3.1(a) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
A doggy door was broken/chewed up and half of it was missing. The chewed up edges don't allow for the doggy door to 
be properly cleaned and sanitized. In addition, there is a big gap that could make it difficult to keep the cold out of that 
enclosure in the winter months. Housing facilities for dogs must be designed and constructed so they are structurally 
sound. They must be kept in good repair. 
 
During inspection, a dog was able to leave one enclosure and enter another enclosure. While the the licensee and I didn't 
observe it, we believe the dog hopped over the separating wall of the enclosure. I did observe the one enclosure to have 
only two dogs one minute and then a minute later the enclosure had three dogs in it. Having enclosures that don't contain 
the animals securely or restrict other animals from entering it could result in dogs getting injured when they are moving 
(jumping over the wall) from one enclosure to the other. Also, a dog entering an enclosure it wasn't intended to be in 
doesn't ensure that dog will be compatible with the dogs that are in that enclosure that it moved (jumped) into. This could 
result in a dog fight and potential injuries or even the death of a dog. The licensee must ensure the enclosures contain all 
the dogs securely and restrict other animals from entering. 
 
Correct by 5/16/26. 
3.11(a)  Repeat

Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
Feces was accumulating throughout most of the enclosures throughout the kennel. When asked, the licensee stated they 
were last cleaned two days ago. The accumulation of feces can contribute to the spread of disease. Excreta must be 
removed from the primary enclosures daily. 
3.11(b)(2) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
At least three doggy doors had grime built up on them. At least three enclosures containing nursing dogs and their 
puppies had grime and feces material smeared onto the floor within the enclosure. The licensee wasn't sure exactly when 
the enclosures were last sanitized but thought it was pushing two weeks. Grime built up on the doggy doors and fecal 
material smeared onto the floor of female dogs and their puppies can contribute to the spread of disease. Used primary 
enclosures must be sanitized every two weeks or more often as necessary to prevent the accumulation of dirt, debris, 
excreta, and other disease hazards. Correct by 5/18/26. 
3.11(c) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
Three empty enclosures had mouse droppings scattered throughout the enclosures. The licensee is not currently using 
those enclosures. The licensee stated he had a mouse problem months ago, but has taken care of that and this feces 
accumulation is from the mouse problem he had months ago. Not cleaning up old mouse feces can contribute to the 
spread of disease within the kennel. All housing facilities must be kept clean to help reduce/eliminate the spread of 
disease. Correct by 5/18/26. 
3.13(a)(3) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
None of the adult dogs are vaccinated against distemper. In addition, none of the adult dogs have been sampled for 
heartworm or fecal parasites (the program of veterinary care calls for 10% of the dogs to be sampled). Not vaccinating 
against distemper and sampling for heartworm or fecal parasites doesn't ensure the population of dogs is adequately 
protected against those diseases/parasites. The licensee must vaccinate all dogs against distemper and sample the 
population for heartworm and fecal parasites in accordance with a schedule approved by the attending veterinarian. 
Correct by 6/15/26. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee. 
 
End of report.
